content.subtitle: Uses
items: Single completed actions
Repeated or habitual actions in the past
General truths about the past
Permanent situations and states in the past
The main events in a story
The present (in conditional sentences)
The present (after wish, it's time, would rather, etc.)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Ancient astronomers **mapped** the night sky with remarkable precision.
As a child, she **walked** through the forest to school every single morning.
Early sailing vessels **were** often fragile and difficult to navigate.
**Did** the first explorers **carry** enough provisions for such long voyages?
Marco **locked** the door and **set off** into the darkness.
If we **didn't have** access to clean water, how would daily life change?
I'd rather the photographer **didn't share** those images without permission.

content.subtitle: Emphatic Past Simple
content.description: To emphasise contrast in the past or strong feeling in the past.
examples: Perhaps explorers of the past didn't have GPS, but they **did have** an extraordinary ability to read the stars.
I **did enjoy** the guided tour of the ancient ruins last spring.

sections.title: Past Simple vs. Present Perfect Simple

content.subtitle: Key Differences
items: Past simple: periods of time or moments which are finished. Present perfect: periods of time which continue up to the present.
Past simple: events at a specific time in the past. Present perfect: specific time isn't important or relevant, or present result is important.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: The twentieth century **witnessed** extraordinary shifts in the way people communicated.
Recent decades **have seen** a dramatic change in attitudes towards environmental responsibility.
I **wrote** my first letter abroad three years ago.
**Have** you ever **written** a handwritten letter before?

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Actions in progress at a particular moment in the past
Actions in progress around a particular moment in the past
Temporary situations and series of actions in the past
Changing and developing situations in the past
Annoying or amusing past habits (usually with always)
Background information in a story
Actions in progress over a period of time
Two actions in progress at the same time
The present and the future (in conditional sentences)
The present and the future (after wish, it's time, would rather, etc.)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: **Were** you **hiking** through the mountains all day last Saturday?
At the close of the nineteenth century, many expeditions **were being launched** to previously unexplored territories.
At the time, she **was studying** marine biology at a university in Barcelona.
I **was getting** increasingly frustrated with the unreliable transport links, so I bought a bicycle.
As a boy, my grandfather **was always collecting** old coins and pressed flowers wherever he went.
A gentle breeze **was blowing** and birds **were settling** into the trees for the night.
**Were** you **preparing** your presentation all yesterday afternoon?
While Clara **was cooking** dinner, her brother **was feeding** the animals outside.
Would you be more content if you **were studying** environmental science?
I wish we **were going** to the nature reserve next weekend.

content.subtitle: Key Differences
items: Past continuous: background events in progress. Past simple: main events.
Past simple: regular or repeated actions in the past (not past continuous).

content.subtitle: Examples
items: We **were talking** about mountain trails when Lucia **mentioned** her new hiking blog.
When I was a teenager, I **visited** my aunt in the countryside every summer.

sections.title: Past Continuous vs. Present Perfect Continuous

content.subtitle: Key Difference
items: Past continuous: actions in progress during periods of time or at moments which are finished.
Present perfect continuous: actions in progress during periods of time which continue up to the present.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: We **were walking** along the coastal path for five hours yesterday.
We **have been walking** along the coastal path for five hours already.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Situations and states before the past
Completed actions before a moment in the past
A series of actions continuing up to a moment in the past
Completed actions where the important thing is the result at a moment in the past

content.subtitle: Examples
items: **Had** you **owned** that painting long before you decided to sell it?
When modern theatres appeared, live performance **had already become** a beloved tradition across society.
When Valentina called, I **had managed** to return all the borrowed books.
By the time of her farewell lecture, Professor Rivera **had taught** hundreds of students who went on to distinguished careers.
I beat Sofia at chess because I **'d practised** the game extensively with my cousin.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions and situations continuing up to a moment in the past (or just before a moment in the past)

content.subtitle: Example
items: He **'d been composing** music for over twelve years before he finally received a major award.

content.subtitle: Would
content.description: Past habits, particularly for the distant past. Can also be used with a continuous infinitive for habits involving actions in progress. Not usually used to refer to past states.
examples: The ancient Romans **would rely on** a vast network of roads to hold their empire together.
Whenever I stayed at my uncle's farmhouse, he **would usually be working** in the garden.

content.subtitle: Used to
content.description: Past habits and states, particularly for the distant past. Not the same as 'be used to' (familiar situation) or 'get used to' (process of becoming familiar).
examples: It **used to seem** remarkable that messages could travel across continents in a matter of moments.
At first, newcomers found it strange adjusting to city life, but now everyone's **used to it**.
It's surprising how quickly travellers in the early twentieth century **got used to** crossing oceans by steamship.

sections.title: A: Past Simple vs. Present Perfect Simple
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Sofia should know about the schedule change because we **told / have told** her about it at last Monday's meeting.
**Did you meet / Have you met** last August while you were both volunteering at the wildlife reserve?
I'm thinking of changing my route because the road **has become / became** much more dangerous over the past few months.
Three weeks ago, **I received / have received** an invitation from the museum, but I haven't replied yet.
Elena **has been / was** eager to speak to you ever since she heard about your discovery.
I **had / have had** absolutely no idea you were such an experienced rock climber.
If you **finished / have finished**, please close your booklets and pass them to the front.
There **were / have been** several important factors behind the failure of the expedition.
It was my graduation last month and my uncle **gave / has given** me a beautiful compass.
I don't know who is responsible because **I didn't work / haven't worked** here for very long.
We **were never allowed / have never been allowed** to stay out after dark when we were children.
The academy **sent / has sent** me to compete at regional tournaments until I was fifteen.

sections.title: B: Past Simple vs. Past Continuous
sections.instructions: Write the verb in brackets in the correct form, past simple or past continuous, in each gap. You may have to use the passive form.

items.sentence: When the storm hit, hundreds of people ...... (shelter) inside the community centre.
As soon as the whistle blew, I ...... (stop) running and turned to look.
Nico ...... (sketch) in his notebook, so he didn't notice the time passing.
She ...... (take) long walks along the cliffs every evening throughout her stay.
We decided to head inside because the temperature ...... (drop) rapidly and we didn't want to get cold.
...... Rafael already ...... (think) of transferring to another department before he failed his assessment?
Since there was a gym nearby, ...... you ...... (exercise) every day?
The expansion of the railways ...... (transform) the way people travelled across the continent.
The ancient monument ...... (discover) by archaeologists working in the region during the 1930s.
I'm sorry I couldn't call you earlier, but I ...... (revise) for my final exam.
By the time we ...... (reach) the summit, the clouds had closed in.
The marine reserve ...... (designate) to protect the coral reef from further damage.

sections.title: C: Gap Fill (Story)
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: It all (1) ...... as I was making my way home after a long shift at the library.
We (2) ...... on a community archive project, and everyone (3) ...... exhausted after weeks of research, so it felt good to finally leave the building.
items.answer: were working / had been working, was feeling

items.sentence: It (4) ...... lightly and it seemed like everyone (5) ...... home at the same time, trying to find shelter from the cold.
I (6) ...... into the train carriage and (7) ...... near the doors when I (8) ...... him at the far end of the compartment.
He (9) ...... a battered leather briefcase that (10) ...... me of something from an old spy film, though I couldn't say exactly why.
As I watched, he (11) ...... the briefcase and (12) ...... out what appeared to be an old rolled document.
He (13) ...... it carefully, then (14) ...... in my direction.
Suddenly, my phone (15) ...... a sound that (16) ...... I had received a new message.
Frowning, I (17) ...... the screen and the text (18) ......
'We (19) ...... your missing files. We know you (20) ...... the map. Wait at the next station.'
I looked up just as he (21) ...... between the carriages.
I (22) ...... to my feet and followed.

sections.title: D: Error Correction
sections.instructions: One verb in each sentence is in the wrong tense. Underline the incorrect word or phrase and write it in the correct tense.

items.sentence: I've been having a lot of trouble with my old camera recently and so I took it to a specialist, but she **has told** me that the lens was cracked and would be expensive to replace.
It **was** almost a week since I heard from Gabriel, but he left Peru last Tuesday and he was due to arrive in Mexico yesterday, so I contacted his sister to check if she had any news.

Clara and I were having a lively debate about environmental policy when Marco suddenly walked over and wanted to join in, even though he **has known** absolutely nothing about what we were discussing.

Nina **had never been visiting** a working harbour before, so when the opportunity came up she was delighted to explore the docks, and she certainly wasn't bored, although they weren't allowed to board any of the vessels.

Leo had no idea what Sarah had said to Emilia, but he could see immediately that she looked upset, so he **was placing** his hand gently on her shoulder to comfort her, which seemed to help enormously.

Although there was no reason to question what Ines had said, I **was calling** Daniel to check, and he confirmed that she had been right and the lecture had been cancelled because the speaker was unwell.

The evening before, I had lent Pedro a book for the journey, but this particular week I wanted to do something special, so I went to the market and was browsing the stalls when all of a sudden I **had seen** the perfect gift.
Mateo was jogging to the park, as he usually did on mornings when the gym was closed, when he **was spotting** an elderly man who was struggling with a heavy bag and looked as though he needed help.

sections.title: E: Past Continuous vs. Present Perfect Continuous
sections.instructions: Write the verb in brackets in the correct form, past continuous or present perfect continuous, in each gap.

items.sentence: 'Why are you out of breath?' 'Because I ...... (run) for the last forty minutes.'
'Your bedroom light was still on when I walked past.' 'Yes, I ...... (study) for my history exam.'
'You've really improved on the guitar.' 'Well, I hope so because I ...... (practise) a lot recently.'
'You're not jealous, are you?' 'No, but you ...... (spend) quite a lot of time with your new colleague lately.'
'I didn't wake you up, did I?' 'No, I ...... (sleep) at all — I was wide awake.'
'Did you ever think about dropping the course?' 'I ...... (consider) it for quite a while, but in the end I decided to stay.'
'Why is the pavement all dug up?' 'Oh, the council ...... (repair) the water pipes, but they should finish this weekend.'

sections.title: F: Past Simple vs. Past Perfect
sections.instructions: Write one verb in each sentence in the past simple and the other verb in the past perfect.

items.sentence: The fire engine ...... (leave) by the time the journalists ...... (arrive) at the scene.
Before I ...... (move) in with my flatmates, I ...... (live) alone for several years.
It ...... (be) only the third time I ...... (ever / travel) by boat.
Up to the moment when Ms Winters ...... (announce) the result, I ...... (have) absolutely no idea what to expect.
When Hugo ...... (finish) eating, he ...... (ask) the waiter whether a service charge was included.
Fortunately, I ...... (just / submit) my assignment when the system ...... (go) offline.
We ...... (pack) all our bags by midday, so we ...... (decide) to leave for the station early.
I ...... (want) the students to touch the display because I ...... (just / arrange) it.
My father ...... (be) disappointed with me because I ...... (forget) to buy bread on my way home.
I ...... (buy) Lena a scarf for her birthday, but she ...... (already / own) a very similar one.

sections.title: G: Error Correction (Text)
sections.instructions: If a word or phrase in bold is in the correct form, write OK. If it is incorrect, write the correct form on the line.

items.sentence: Photography (1) **has transformed** the way humans record and share their experiences.
In the mid-nineteenth century, the technology (2) **had seemed** so astonishing that early photographers (3) **were compared** to magicians by many ordinary people.
Since those first experiments, however, photography (4) **became** central to daily life and culture.
Although cameras (5) **were initially used** only in expensive professional studios, photographic technology (6) **had spread** to reach ordinary families within just a few decades.
The impact on how people remember their lives and relationships (7) **had been** immense.

We have grown accustomed to documenting every important moment with a photograph. Before this was possible, there were occasions when significant events (8) **went** undocumented, and many people (9) **had** no visual record of those who came before them.
As photography (10) **has been becoming** more sophisticated, so it (11) **has become** more affordable.
The large, fragile camera of the Victorian era (12) **has evolved** into the compact digital device of today.

sections.title: H: Sentence Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
I began studying at eight in the morning and was still at my desk at ten that night when you rang.
**been** When you rang, ...... fourteen hours.

In our childhood, we often went hiking in the mountains with our neighbours.
**would** Our neighbours ...... in the mountains as children.

It is almost five years now since Carla began to learn Japanese.
**learning** Carla ...... five years.
items.answer: has been learning Japanese for almost

items.sentence:
Sharing a flat with strangers was uncomfortable at first, but I eventually stopped noticing the differences.
**used** Sharing a flat felt strange at first, but I ...... with flatmates.

Each evening for the past fortnight I have had the same strange dream.
**having** I ...... for a fortnight now.
items.answer: have been having the same dream

items.sentence:
I never enjoyed early morning runs, but now I am starting to look forward to them.
**used** I ...... early morning runs, but now I'm starting to.

sections.title: I: Gap Fill (Text - The Map)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Because maps (1) ...... become such an essential part of how people navigate the world, it is hard to believe that accurate geographical charts (2) ...... not always existed in the form we know today.
Before the age of exploration, travellers (3) ...... to rely on rough, hand-drawn sketches that were riddled with errors.
It (4) ...... considered perfectly acceptable for cartographers to fill unknown territories with invented creatures or to leave vast areas blank.
Up to that point, no one (5) ...... managed to produce a complete and reliable picture of the entire globe.
Then, in the fifteenth century, a large body of ancient geographical knowledge (6) ...... rediscovered by European scholars.
Explorers (7) ...... consult these newly recovered texts alongside their own observations to produce far more accurate charts.
Soon, word of these improved maps (8) ...... spread to merchants and navigators across the continent, who were eager to use them for planning long voyages.
Maps are difficult to keep accurate, and in order to be truly reliable they (9) ...... to be revised and updated as new discoveries were made.
Cartographers had (10) ...... working to improve geographical accuracy for many decades before the first comprehensive atlas was finally produced.
How long ...... before the rescue team finally arrived?
You ...... hate living in the countryside, didn't you?
Could you explain to me what exactly you ...... in the archive room at midnight?
I was exhausted when you called because I ...... for hours.
The local team ...... three matches already this season.
Don't turn off the laptop because I ...... my work yet.
By the time we got to the station, the last train ......
We had to postpone the trip because Clara ...... her passport.
It took Sofia a while to ...... teaching so many students at once.
I'd love to have grown up in a small village, where children ...... freely through the fields.
I much preferred it when our family ...... on camping trips every August.
His coach called me today and mentioned that he ...... during training again.
